item.title: Topolski's Chronicle, V.4 No.7-8, 1956
item.creator: Topolski, Feliks, 1907-1989
item.description: Topolski's Chronicle is a series of broadsheets created by Feliks Topolski and distributed bimonthly to subscribers from 1953 through 1982. Topolski was a superlative draughtsman and covered current events with an artistic, unique style.

pages.typeset_text:
TOPOLSKI's CHRONICLE No. 7-8 (67-68) Vol IV 1956
...nothing affirmeth and therefore never lyeth... Sir Phillip Sidney (1580)

658
A MYSTIC'S EPISTLE by SWAMI AGEHANANDA BHARATI
Swami Agehananda wrote this epistle in reply to the CHRONICLE'S request for a dissertation on Indian Mysticism. He chose, sadly, to ponder over What Mysticism Is Not and, then, in the customary Swami manner, vanished, no doubt to continue in peace his sadhanas. But, whatever the disappointments of his text, it seems worthy of publication for the vigour and originality of its exotic iconoclasm.
The quest after happiness is the chief quest, though the mystic life; and I warn you that professional mystics, in phrase is hackneyed. I am writing to you attained by normal human beings, and the clique of those about a particular happiness only, the happiness of the who teach its attainment is so small that competitors from without hardly come forth. What the mystic promises, cannot be refuted, though it does not have to be accepted. Non-acceptance, on the other hand, adds to the strength of the mystic's promise, for it makes the circle of the elect more elect, and non-acceptance more vulgar. One of my homiletic pastimes is to let little secrets leak out from the mystics' hermetic sphere, for critical thought and philosophers are my secret love-secret, because the mystics don't like the philosophers, unless the latter claim to be mystics of a special kind-like the metaphysicians; their endeavour the professional mystics condone, because it is harmless. The classical metaphysician writes about intuition of the Absolute, about the non-discursive method of that intuition: it is a sheer joy to the mystic who has this intuition, to whom it is an entirely simple affair (simple, that is, once he has attained it), and to whom the verbose disquisitions of the teachers of metaphysics, if they ever get acquainted with them, sound like utter and self-complacent swindle. UNIVERSITY OF UTAH LIBRARIES
The drawings in this issue were made in India, mostly in Banaras.
sketch_descriptions: A rough expressive line drawing of a seated bald figure in the center, surrounded by a dense jungle or forest with tall palm trees and large leafy plants.
Behind the seated figure is a jagged mountain or large rock with a zigzag path or line leading up to a small structure or temple at the peak.
pages.page_summary: This 1956 Topolski's Chronicle page features an article titled 'A Mystic's Epistle' by Swami Agehananda Bharati discussing Indian mysticism, accompanied by an expressive sketch of a mystic figure in a jungle landscape with mountains and trees. The drawings were reportedly made in India, mainly in Banaras.

sketch_descriptions: Top left drawing of a street scene with a figure leaning out of a window above and several people sitting along stairs and near a doorway, with birds flying and boat scenes in the background, including a person rowing a boat and another seated under an umbrella.

Top right drawing featuring multiple human figures positioned on steps or blocks, one larger figure seated with other standing figures around including one with hands behind the head, a few birds flying around.

Bottom left sketch illustrating a waterfront scene with boats on the water, several figures on structures by the water's edge, and groups of people gathered with some standing and others seated, a bird flying overhead.
Bottom right drawing of a crowded scene with many standing and seated figures, some under a canopy or shaded structure, and others grouped along what appears to be steps or tiers.
pages.page_summary: This page contains four distinct ink sketches depicting lively social and waterfront scenes with multiple human figures engaging in various activities such as boating, resting on steps, and gathering under umbrellas or canopies.
